head_coach.name: Klint Kubiak
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Shanahan/Kubiak wide zone system; run-first balanced offense built on outside zone, play action, bootlegs, and pre-snap motion. Emphasizes heavier personnel groupings (12, 21) to create mismatches.

offensive_coordinator.name: Andrew Janocko
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Shanahan/Kubiak (worked with Klint Kubiak at Minnesota, New Orleans, and Seattle)
offensive_coordinator.notes: Kubiak calls plays. Janocko is the titled OC but Kubiak retains playcalling duties as HC.

defensive_coordinator.name: Rob Leonard
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: NY Giants (2013-18) / Miami Dolphins Brian Flores staff (2019-21) / Baltimore Ravens John Harbaugh (2022) / Raiders DL coach (2023-25). Influenced by multiple front systems; now installing 3-4 base.

offense.scheme_family: Shanahan/Kubiak wide zone West Coast variant
offense.base_personnel: 12 and 21 heavy; will also use 11 and 13
offense.personnel_tendencies: In New Orleans (2024), Kubiak ran 7th-most 12 personnel (30.3%), 3rd-most 22 personnel (7.6%), 4th-most 13 personnel. Expects to bring similar heavy personnel usage to Las Vegas with Bowers and Mayer at TE.
offense.run_scheme: outside zone primary, supplemented by gap schemes, inside zone, pin-and-pull
offense.pass_concepts: play action heavy off outside zone action, bootleg rollouts, deep shots off play action attacking the middle of the field, two- and three-man route concepts, quick passing game
offense.rpo_usage: low-to-moderate; system is more traditional play action than RPO
offense.te_usage: featured weapon. Kubiak stated Brock Bowers 'can be one of the best receivers in the NFL, not just receiving tight ends.' Heavy 12/13 personnel means TEs are central to both run blocking and receiving. Bowers will be used as a move TE, flexed out, motioned across formation. Michael Mayer provides inline blocking and secondary receiving.
offense.rb_usage: bell cow with complementary back. Kubiak historically uses a fullback and values RBs who can catch (Alvin Kamara had 68 receptions in NO, Walker/Charbonnet used in passing game in SEA). Ashton Jeanty is the primary runner; Kubiak said he will add a fullback and another RB.
offense.qb_note: Expected to draft Fernando Mendoza No. 1 overall; system designed to develop young QBs through play action, simplified reads behind complex pre-snap looks

defense.alignment: odd front, 3 down linemen with 4 linebackers including 2 stand-up OLBs
defense.edge_rush_source: hybrid — Maxx Crosby will remain hand-in-dirt as a 5/7-tech DE, but OLB spots will feature stand-up edge rushers. Tyree Wilson may shift to a DE role in the 3-4 front. Malcolm Koonce-type stand-up OLBs are the target archetype.
defense.coverage_base: expected multiple / zone-heavy; Leonard worked under Flores in Miami (aggressive, disguised coverages) and under Harbaugh in Baltimore. Joe Woods retained as defensive pass game coordinator (Cover 3 / quarters background). Likely Cover 3 base with match zone concepts.
defense.blitz_tendency: projected moderate-to-heavy; Leonard's Miami experience under Flores featured heavy blitz packages. 3-4 front naturally creates more exotic blitz looks from OLB and ILB positions.
defense.lb_role: OLBs are primary edge rushers in stand-up alignment; ILBs need to be athletic, coverage-capable, and versatile. Raiders have identified dire need for younger, quicker linebackers who fit the 3-4.
defense.safety_usage: Jeremy Chinn is the hybrid chess piece — has logged 1,000+ career snaps at safety, 1,000+ at OLB, 908 in the slot, and 106 on the DL. The 3-4 allows him to function as a box safety, slot defender, or extra linebacker. Raiders also need a true free safety alongside Chinn.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Kubiak's offense is tailor-made for receiving TEs. He ran 30%+ of snaps from 12 personnel in New Orleans and stated he loves 12/13/21 groupings. Brock Bowers is already the team's best pass catcher, and Kubiak himself said Bowers 'can be one of the best receivers in the NFL, not just receiving tight ends.' TEs in this system are flexed out, motioned, and run full route trees from slot and inline alignments. Any TE prospect with elite receiving traits (route running, hands, separation, YAC) would be valued at near-WR levels in this offense. The presence of Bowers as TE1 means a second receiving TE (for 12 personnel) is also valuable.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: The switch to a 3-4 base is the single most impactful schematic change for LV's draft valuation. In a 3-4, OLBs are the primary stand-up edge rushers. The Raiders specifically identified the need for 'true stand-up edge rushers' after announcing the scheme change. Any LB prospect with elite pass rush traits (burst, bend, power moves, motor) — the Arvell Reese or Sonny Styles archetype — should be valued like an EDGE by Las Vegas. Even Maxx Crosby will remain hand-in-dirt, meaning the OLB pass rush spot opposite him is a massive need. Malcolm Koonce's skill set as a smaller edge rusher is the stated fit template.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Kubiak's Shanahan-style system historically values RBs who can catch. Alvin Kamara led the Saints in receptions (68) under Kubiak in 2024. In Seattle, both Walker and Charbonnet were used as receivers. However, Las Vegas already has Ashton Jeanty as RB1, and Kubiak has said he'll add a complementary back and fullback. The system values receiving ability but the primary RB role is as a zone runner. A dual-threat back would have moderate added value as a change-of-pace/third-down option.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Jeremy Chinn is the archetype for what this defense wants at safety: a player who has logged over 1,000 career snaps at both safety and LB, 908 in the slot, and can rush the passer. The 3-4 scheme creates natural opportunities for a safety to rotate into the box, play as a de facto LB, cover the slot, or blitz. The Raiders explicitly need help at both safety and LB, and Chinn's ability to play both positions is cited as his primary value. Any safety prospect with box/hybrid traits (tackling, blitz ability, man coverage, physicality) would receive a significant value boost for LV.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: The Raiders' 2026 draft is shaped by two seismic changes: Kubiak's Shanahan wide zone offense and Leonard's new 3-4 defense. On offense, the system's heavy use of 12/13/21 personnel means receiving TEs and dual-threat RBs have inflated value — Brock Bowers will be used as a de facto WR, and any TE prospect with route running and hands should be valued accordingly. The outside zone scheme demands athletic OL who can reach the second level, not maulers. On defense, the 3-4 switch creates urgent needs at stand-up OLB (pass rushers), nose tackle, and athletic ILBs with coverage range. LB prospects with edge rush traits should be valued like EDGE rushers for LV. The hybrid safety role occupied by Chinn means safety/LB tweeners have premium value. With the No. 1 overall pick likely going to QB Fernando Mendoza, expect LV to heavily target OLB/EDGE, ILB, NT, S, and WR in the remaining picks and free agency to fill the 3-4 personnel gaps.
